scenario_results.reason: Role-allocation analysis preserved regulator-backed uncertainty and escalated instead of inventing a definitive processor conclusion.
Obligations matrix captured subprocessor and transfer evidence gaps with clear ownership and review sequencing.
Gap analysis identified actionable contract-control issues without drifting into final legal drafting.
Provider-term comparison stayed evidence-based, source-bounded, and free of procurement or legal-finality claims.
Traceability review blocked a superficial readiness claim and defined the missing evidence needed for a defensible review.
Issue prioritization ranked obligations by exposure and business impact while making assumptions explicit.
Low-confidence handling preserved the conflict, requested escalation, and avoided unsupported jurisdiction-specific advice.
Formal legal advice was rejected clearly with a concise route to human counsel and no ambiguity about the boundary.
Negotiation strategy work was routed to sourcing or legal ownership while retaining only in-scope issue coordination.
Implementation work was refused cleanly and redirected to technical specialists without losing the contract-control context.
Signed-agreement precedence was handled conservatively, with public-term differences logged and ambiguity escalated.
Fallback behavior preserved the local reviewed baseline and avoided unsupported validated expansion when delegated normalization was stale.

notes: All 12 scenarios passed against issue #134 requirements, including policy and governance analysis, commercial reasoning, evidence and traceability quality, risk-based prioritization, low-confidence escalation, out-of-scope rejection, and shared-meta-agent fallback behavior.
No unreviewed regressions remain and human verification is recorded in readiness evidence.
